title: SA Asks: What are the most attractive upcoming IPOs?
content:
[IPO Concept with Stock Market Graphs]

What are the most attractive upcoming initial public offerings in the wake of SpaceX's (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) spectacular market debut?

Seeking Alpha analysts Donovan Jones [https://seekingalpha.com/author/donovan-jones], Brett Ashcroft Green [https://seekingalpha.com/author/brett-ashcroft-green], and Eugenio Catone [https://seekingalpha.com/author/eugenio-catone] weigh in. 

_Donovan Jones,_ [https://seekingalpha.com/author/donovan-jones]_ leader of SA investing group __IPO Edge_: [https://seekingalpha.com/checkout/mp_1225] With SpaceX's (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) successful market debut indicating plenty of dry powder for strong narrative IPOs, a number of other companies are braving the IPO market, seeking to obtain their share of investor funds while hoping that SpaceX doesn’t pull all the investing energy, or dollars, from their offerings.

Kardigan (KARD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/KARD]) is a clinical-stage biopharma developing treatments for various serious cardiovascular diseases. The firm, which made its market debut on Thursday, is backed by well-regarded institutional life science investors.

Bending Spoons (BSP [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BSP]), meanwhile, may be an interesting target for digital media investors as it's been acquiring legacy digital media properties with the intent of revitalizing and using them as cash-generating portfolio companies to fund further acquisitions.

On the tech side, OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) and Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]) are likely to launch soon, with high demand for their shares, as they lead the generative AI race. Both firms have recently raised large private investment rounds at valuations approaching $1T, but face challenges.

OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) has seen its consumer focus produce less revenue and usage growth than Anthropic’s (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]) enterprise/coding focus. However, Anthropic’s latest Mythos 5 flagship release has been removed from the market pending further review by U.S. regulators, casting doubt on its ability to reliably and predictably produce upgrades that won’t be pulled from the market on a moment’s notice.

_Brett Ashcroft Green [https://seekingalpha.com/author/brett-ashcroft-green]__:_ For myself personally, SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]), and Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]) are all attractive. The issue with most of these, however, is that they’ve gone through so many series of fundraisings on the private market that we retail investors have to pay highly inflated prices. 

Pre-SpaceX IPO, I, personally, took a position in BPTRX (BPTRX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BPTRX]), the Baron Partners mutual fund. This has about 33% exposure to SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]). The fund is up about 20% for June with adjustments pre- and post-SpaceX IPO. 

For now, SpaceX is in a league of its own and will be for some time. Regardless of valuation and multiples, this should carry some value in and of itself until Blue Origin (BORGN [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BORGN]) achieves regular satellite launches.

With SpaceX’s intent to acquire Cursor, the company now seems to have an immediate entry into the LLM debate vs. OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) and Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]). The major LLMs have the advantage of being an underlying engine to a lot of code editors, including Cursor. If the narrative that SpaceX's Grok and xAI can be a viable option to these in the run-up to their IPOs, this could ultimately dampen investor demand for their shares, in my opinion.

_Eugenio Catone_ [https://seekingalpha.com/author/eugenio-catone]_:_ The AI narrative is stronger than ever, hype is sky-high, and this is something that major AI private companies know really well. It is not a coincidence that they all planned to go public this year, and I am talking primarily about SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]), OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]), and the SK Hynix ADR. 

To the question, "What are the most attractive upcoming IPOs?," I believe the most logical answer is none of these. They all present unreasonable valuations, and, in my opinion, these IPOs are merely a strategy to cash out after such a massive rally. My suggestion for retail investors is not to take the bait.

title: Kardigan shares soar after Nasdaq debut
content:
Investing.com -- Kardigan Inc. shares rose iver 27% roughly one hour into its Nasdaq debut on Thursday, after the heart-health focused biotech raised $400 million in an initial public offering.

The company's shares opened at $16.25 each as of 12:39 p.m. in New York, above its IPO price of $16 apiece, and were trading above $20.30 by approximately 1:30 p.m. that day.

The trading gives the company a market value of nearly $1.8 billion, based on the outstanding shares.

The heart-health focused biotech sold 25 million shares at $16 each, the company said Wednesday. The firm had marketed 23.3 million shares for $14 to $16 each, an earlier filing showed.

Kardigan's Chief Executive Officer Tassos Gianakakos previously served as the CEO of MyoKardia. That company was acquired for about $13 billion by Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. in 2020. Kardigan is developing separate drugs to treat genetic dilated cardiomyopathy, calcific aortic valve stenosis and acute severe hypertension.

CEO Gianakakos said the growing boom in GLP-1s, a class of weight loss drugs, will not reduce the need for better heart disease drugs.

Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death in the U.S., according to the American Heart Association. The organization counts the growing prevalence of high blood pressure, diabetes and obesity as contributors to increased risk.

Kardigan had a net loss of $56.1 million in the first quarter of 2026, compared with a net loss of $18 million in the same period a year earlier, the filing shows.

In October, Kardigan raised $254 million from a Series B financing round backed by new investors Fidelity Management & Research Co. and accounts advised by T. Rowe Price Investment Management Inc. and existing backers ARCH Venture Partners and HRTG Partners. Arch and HRTG are Kardigan's two biggest shareholders, with 22.4% each, a previous filing showed.

The offering was led by JPMorgan Chase & Co., Jefferies Financial Group Inc., Leerink Partners and Toronto-Dominion Bank.

The shares now trade on the Nasdaq Global Market under the symbol KARD.

title: Cardiovascular biotech Kardigan prices upsized $400M IPO at $16/share
content:
[IPO Initial Public Offering 3D Graph Chart Background]
filo/E+ via Getty Images

Kardigan (KARD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/KARD]), a clinical-stage precision therapeutics company specializing in unaddressed cardiovascular diseases, has announced the pricing of its initial public offering (IPO) [https://seekingalpha.com/pr/20555947-kardigan-announces-pricing-of-upsized-initial-public-offering] of 25M shares of common stock at a public offering price of $16.00 per share.

Kardigan initially [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4602684-cardiology-biotech-kardigan-seeks-370m-us-ipo] planned to sell 23.3M shares targeting a raise of approximately $350M (or up to $373.3M at the very top end of its $14-to-$16 range). 

By expanding the deal, total gross proceeds from the primary offering are now projected to be $400M before underwriting discounts, commissions, and transaction expenses.

The company's common stock is scheduled to begin trading today, June 18, 2026, on the Nasdaq Global Market under the ticker symbol "KARD."

Kardigan has granted the underwriting syndicate a standard 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 3.75M shares at the initial public offering price, less underwriting discounts.

The offering is anticipated to officially close on June 22, 2026, subject to the satisfaction of customary regulatory closing conditions.

Headquartered in South San Francisco, California, and Princeton, New Jersey, Kardigan develops new medicines for specific cardiovascular diseases lacking approved therapies. They combine cardiovascular biology, patient data, and analytics for precision cardiology treatments.

J.P. Morgan Securities LLC, Jefferies LLC, Leerink Partners LLC, and TD Securities (USA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/USA]) LLC are serving as the joint book-running managers for the offering.

title: Kardigan targets $1.4 billion valuation in U.S. IPO
content:
Kardigan filed for a U.S. initial public offering targeting a valuation of up to $1.4 billion, the Princeton, New Jersey-based clinical-stage biotech said on Thursday. Its offering consists of 23.3 million shares in a $14-to-$16 price range, with gross proceeds potentially reaching $373.3 million.

If shares are sold at the midpoint price of $15, Kardigan expects to raise about $320.3 million after expenses. If underwriters buy an extra 3.5 million shares, that number could increase to around $369.1 million. In that case, the total number of shares would be just under 91.2 million, giving the company a market value of about $1.37 billion at the $15 price.

Kardigan is focusing on three late-stage cardiovascular programs: danicamtiv for genetic dilated cardiomyopathy, ataciguat for calcific aortic valve stenosis, and tonlamarsen, which is being tested for acute severe hypertension. With the money from the IPO and its current cash, the company expects to be funded through 2028.

Spending on research and development surged to $45.1 million in the first quarter, more than twice the year-ago figure, as clinical activity intensified across all three programs. Proceeds from the listing will be directed toward advancing the clinical trials for each of the three candidates, with the remainder allocated to research activities, working capital, and broader corporate needs.

Shares will trade on the Nasdaq Global Market with the symbol "KARD," with the offering managed by J.P. Morgan, Jefferies, Leerink Partners, and TD Cowen.

The offering arrives as the biotech IPO market shows signs of renewed activity. Parabilis Medicines, which raised $670 million in what Fierce Biotech called the largest IPO in biotech history, began trading on Nasdaq the day before Kardigan's announcement, with its stock rising on its first day. According to Reuters, IPOX Research Associate Lukas Muehlbauer noted that the sector's IPO window has reopened, though he warned against drawing comparisons to the 2020-2021 boom era, arguing that capital is flowing only to companies with demonstrated clinical momentum and a credible regulatory runway.

title: Kardigan targets IPO to support cardiology pipeline progression
content:
Just under a year and a half after its debut, Kardigan is eyeing a listing on the Nasdaq to support the continued development of its in-licensed cardiology pipeline – adding another name to the burgeoning list of American biotechs undertaking IPOs this year.

The ARCH Venture Partners-backed company will trade under the ticker “KARD.” While Kardigan has not yet revealed the number or price of shares it will offer, it did describe where the funds will be used. Primarily, proceeds will help progress three of the biotech’s pipeline cardiovascular therapies through the clinic.

Kardigan’s assets include danicamtiv, a MyoKardia and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMS)-licensed candidate to treat genetic dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) – a disease characterised by the heart’s struggle to effectively pump blood around the body. The direct myosin activator is currently under evaluation in a Phase IIb/III trial within the condition. If approved, it could become the first therapy to reach the DCM market.

Alongside danicamtiv development efforts, Kardigan plans todirect funds to its other BMS-MyoKardia-licensed therapy, ataciguat, which is currently in mid-stage clinical trials for the treatment of moderate calcific aortic valve stenosis.

On top of these programmes, Kardigan plans to push its high blood pressure-targeting antisense oligonucleotide, tonlamarsen, further along clinical development. The drug is currently in a Phase IIb trial, which is looking at its potential in patients with acute, severe cases of the condition.

Kardigan will use any leftover cash to buy, in-license or invest in assets, businesses, technologies or products related to its core focus.

Kardigan’s IPO plans come a little after the company raised $254m in an ARCH-backed Series B financing round back in October 2025, which contributed to funding the clinical development of danicamtiv, ataciguat and tonlamarsen.

US sees biotech IPO surge

As Kardigan files its intent to IPO, it joins the growing list of biotechs eyeing a place on the public market in 2026, as listing activity sees a notable uptick year-on-year (YoY).

According to GlobalData’s Pharmaceutical Intelligence Center, the number of IPOs announced in Q1 2026 jumped more than 80% compared with the same period in 2025.

Notable examples in 2026 include Eikon Therapeutics’ $381.2m raise in January, as well as Generate Biomedicines’ $400m raise in February.

After a flurry of IPOs in Q1, Q2 has maintained momentum. Kailera Therapeutics made history in April by securing one of the largest IPOs in the sector’s history valued at $625m.

Story Continues

"Kardigan targets IPO to support cardiology pipeline progression" was originally created and published by Pharmaceutical Technology, a GlobalData owned brand.
